title: Psalms 107
subtitle: Give Thanks to the LORD, for He Is Good

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — The Redeemed Return

sections.header: Verses 1–22 — “Let the Redeemed of the LORD Tell Their Story”

heb.paragraph: "Let the redeemed (gəʾūlē) of the LORD tell their story" (v.2). BOOK V OPENS (Psalms 107–150). The refrain psalm: four groups in trouble cry to God, and four times the psalm responds: "Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them" (vv.6, 13, 19, 28). "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his unfailing love" (vv.8, 15, 21, 31). The four groups: wanderers in the desert (vv.4–9), prisoners in darkness (vv.10–16), fools made sick by sin (vv.17–22), and sailors in a storm (vv.23–32).

notes.note: "Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his love endures forever. Let the redeemed of the LORD tell their story." The opening: gratitude + testimony. The redeemed have a story; they must tell it.
The first group: wanderers. Hungry, thirsty, life ebbing away. They cried; he delivered. He led them by a straight way to a city where they could settle.
The second group: prisoners. Darkness, deepest gloom, iron chains. They cried; he brought them out. He broke the gates of bronze and cut through bars of iron.
The third group: fools sick from sin. Their sin made them ill; they loathed all food. They cried; he sent his WORD and healed them. "Let them sacrifice thank offerings."

refs.note: Gathering from east and west, north and south parallels the psalm's redeemed-from-all-lands imagery.
People coming from east and west to sit at the feast echoes the psalm's gathering theme.
The great multitude from every nation fulfills the psalm's universal redemption vision.

hist.context: Psalm 107 opens Book V (Pss 107-150) with thanksgiving for deliverance. Four scenarios describe those God rescues: desert wanderers (vv.4-9), prisoners (vv.10-16), the sick (vv.17-22), and storm-tossed sailors (vv.23-32). Each group cries to the LORD in trouble and is delivered. The refrain 'let them give thanks' punctuates each section.

sections.header: Verses 23–43 — “He Stilled the Storm to a Whisper”

heb.paragraph: "He stilled the storm to a whisper (dəmāmâ); the waves of the sea were hushed" (v.29). The fourth group: sailors. Waves mount to the heavens and plunge to the depths; courage melts; they reel like drunkards (vv.25–27). They cried; he stilled the storm. He guided them to their desired haven (v.30). The psalm then shifts to wisdom reflection (vv.33–43): God turns rivers into desert and desert into pools; he settles the hungry there. "Let the one who is wise heed these things and ponder the loving deeds of the LORD" (v.43).

notes.note: The fourth group: sailors. "They went up to the heavens and down to the depths; in their peril their courage melted away. They cried; he stilled the storm to a whisper." The storm-stilling: Jesus in Mark 4:39 ("Quiet! Be still!") enacts Psalm 107.
"Let them give thanks to the LORD for his unfailing love. Let them exalt him in the assembly of the people." The fourth refrain. Each rescue demands public testimony.
"Let the one who is wise heed these things and ponder the loving deeds of the LORD." The psalm’s closing instruction: wisdom means noticing God’s pattern of rescue. Pay attention.

refs.note: Jesus calming the storm fulfills the psalm's vision of God stilling the tempest.
The storm at sea and sailors' fear parallels this psalm's nautical crisis.
Paul's shipwreck and deliverance follows the psalm's pattern of maritime rescue.

hist.context: The sea imagery (vv.23-30) is particularly vivid: waves mounting to heaven and plunging to depths, courage melting, staggering like drunkards. God's control over seas demonstrated in creation continues in historical rescue. The wisdom conclusion (vv.33-43) interprets these deliverances as evidence of divine love, calling the wise to ponder them.

ppl.text: " The psalm’s closing instruction: wisdom means noticing God’s pattern of rescue…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVthose he gathered from the lands, from east and west, from north and south.ESVand gathered in from the lands, from the east and from the west, from the north and from the south.NIVLet the redeemed of the Lord tell their story— those he redeemed from the hand of the foe,ESVLet the redeemed of the LORD say so, whom he has redeemed from trouble

rec.text: ""Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his love endures forever. Let the redeemed of the LORD tell their story." The opening: gratitude + testimony. The redeemed have a story; they must tell it."

rows.label: The Storm Stilled — 107:23–43
rows.text: "He stilled the storm to a WHISPER." Sailors at their wits’ end. "Let the wise PONDER."

hebtext.note: "Let the redeemed (gəʾūlē) of the LORD tell their story" (v.2). BOOK V OPENS (Psalms 107–150). The refrain psalm: four groups in trouble cry to God, and four times the psalm responds: "Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them" (vv.6, 13, 19, 28). "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his unfailing love" (vv.8, 15, 21, 31). The four groups: wanderers in the desert (vv.4–9), prisoners in darkness (vv.10–16), fools made sick by sin (vv.17–22), and sailors in a storm (vv.23–32).

themes.note: Mercy peaks: four rescues, four refrains of ḥesed. Sovereignty: storm-stiller. Book V opens with the pattern of cry → rescue → thanksgiving.
